### 1. Facts given in the task
• The complete bibliographic citation for the source:  
  – Author: David Greetham  
  – Article title: "Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)"  
  – Journal title: Textual Cultures: Texts, Contexts, Interpretation  
  – Volume and issue: vol. 3 no. 1  
  – Year of publication: 2008  
  – Page numbers: p. 45-46  
  – Publisher/platform: Project MUSE  
  – DOI: 10.2979/tex.2008.3.1.44

• The in-line citation text that is claimed to appear in the article:  
  “obscured not by a "cloak of print" but by the veil of scribal confusion and mis-transmission”

• The question to decide whether the in-line quotation matches exactly what appears in the article.  
  – If it matches, answer “Yes.”  
  – If not, identify the one word (without any accompanying article) from the citation that does not match.

• The instruction that all relevant tools are available and verification steps should be run to confirm the answer.

### 2. Facts to look up
• The original text of the article on Project MUSE:  
  – Source location: The article “Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)” (2008) by David Greetham; access via Project MUSE or using the DOI: 10.2979/tex.2008.3.1.44.  
  – Specific pages to review: pages 45-46 where the quotation is supposed to appear.

• The precise wording of the cited sentence in the article:  
  – Compare the quoted text provided in the in-line citation to the text on pages 45-46 of the article.  
  – Verification source: the exact text from the digital version (or scanned copy) of the article from Project MUSE.

### 3. Facts to derive
• Comparison result:  
  – Derive, through textual comparison, whether the in-line quotation exactly matches the original text from pages 45-46 of the article.

• Identification of discrepancies:  
  – If a discrepancy is found, isolate the exact single word in the in-line citation that does not match the corresponding word in the original text (reporting it without any accompanying article such as “a”, “an”, or “the”).  
  – If no discrepancy is found, derive the answer “Yes” indicating a perfect match.

• Logical conclusion based on verified textual evidence:  
  – Determine the final answer based solely on the verified content from the look-up step and logical comparison.

steps.original_output:
1. Short answer  
There is no sentence that contains the phrase “cloak of print” in the pages provided. (There is, however, a sentence on pages 45–46 that contains “veil of print.”)

2. Extremely detailed answer  
After carefully reviewing pages 45–46 of David Greetham’s article “Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)” (2008) as provided from Project MUSE, no sentence includes the exact phrase “cloak of print.” What does appear in the text is a sentence with the phrase “veil of print.” That sentence reads as follows (with a slight uncertainty about typographical breaks stemming from pagination):  

  “…scured not by a “veil of print” but by the veil of scribal confusion and mis-transmission. Our/my author lay beyond this confusion, immanent as the lodestar for my arcane models.”  

It appears that the query you posed may be referring to “cloak of print” when the text actually uses “veil of print.” If your intention was indeed to extract a sentence containing “cloak of print,” then unfortunately such a sentence is not found in the provided document. If, however, you meant the sentence with “veil of print,” then the full sentence extends from where “veil of print” appears through the following clause. (Be aware that the sentence might be split over the page break, so in the published version it likely appears as one continuous sentence.)  

3. Additional Context on the document and question asked  
The file you provided is an excerpt from David Greetham’s scholarly article “Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)” published in Textual Cultures in Spring 2008 on Project MUSE. The article discusses Greetham’s experiences and evolving approaches in textual criticism and the notion of authorial presence versus absence in editing practices. Your question specifically requested extraction of a sentence containing “cloak of print” from pages 45–46. However, after close examination the phrase “cloak of print” does not occur—instead, the article uses “veil of print” in a sentence that contrasts the surface appearance of text (the “veil of print”) with the underlying “scribal confusion and mis-transmission.” As such, if you intended to refer to that sentence, the answer provided above is what appears in the document; otherwise, there is no sentence matching “cloak of print.”

1. Short answer  
The sentence is: “Obscured not by a “veil of print” but by the veil of scribal confusion and mis‐transmission.”  

2. Extremely detailed answer  
In the pages provided (pages 45–46 of David Greetham’s “Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)” published in Textual Cultures 3.1, Spring 2008), there is a sentence on page 46 that reads as follows (noting that in the supplied text the first few letters appear truncated as “scured” but are clearly part of the word “Obscured”):  

  “Obscured not by a “veil of print” but by the veil of scribal confusion and mis‐transmission.”  

It is important to note that while your question asks for the sentence containing the phrase “cloak of print,” the document instead uses the phrase “veil of print.” There is no occurrence of “cloak of print” in the provided pages; therefore, it is reasonable to assume that “veil of print” is the intended expression. This single sentence employs a metaphor to contrast the idea of graphical or printed obscuration with the deeper, more complex obscuration caused by scribal errors and mis‐transmissions.

3. Additional Context on the document and question asked  
The document is an excerpt from David Greetham’s article “Uncoupled: OR, How I Lost My Author(s)” (2008), which explores the author’s evolving relationship with textual scholarship and the challenges of editorial interpretation. In these pages, Greetham reflects on how the “author”—as a guiding presence in textual criticism—is not simply hidden behind the physical or apparent “print” (or what might be metaphorically called a “cloak” or “veil”), but rather by the more intricate and messy processes of scribal transmission. Your request to extract the sentence with “cloak of print” seems to be targeting this metaphor; however, the actual text uses “veil of print.” The answer provided here quotes the complete sentence containing that metaphor from the specified pages, preserving its original meaning and imagery.
